title: Sunday, July 29
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## Sermon Date: July 29, 2018 | Preacher: Katie Pearson

LITURGICAL CONTEXT
July 29, 2018 falls on the Eighth Sunday after Pentecost (Proper 12 in the Revised Common Lectionary). The lectionary readings for this date would likely include 2 Kings 4:42-44 (Elisha feeding the hundred men), Psalm 145, Ephesians 3:14-21, and John 6:1-21 (Jesus feeding the five thousand and walking on water). The church was in Ordinary Time, the longest liturgical season, with focus on Jesus's teachings and miracles during his ministry.

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS
The Episcopal Church was in the immediate aftermath of General Convention 2018, held in Austin, Texas just days earlier (July 5-13), which had addressed issues including human sexuality, immigration, and constitutional revisions. Bishop Robert Wright of the Diocese of Colorado was in the second year of his tenure after being elected in 2017. The General Convention's debates on sexuality and marriage had generated significant discussion within Episcopal parishes nationwide, including likely within Saint John's Cathedral community.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS
Denver was experiencing a typical hot, dry July with temperatures in the 80s-90s Fahrenheit. Locally, attention was focused on preparations for the 2018 midterm elections in November, with Colorado's Senate race between Democrat John Hickenlooper and Republican Cory Gardner shaping up as one of the nation's most competitive races. The downtown Denver and Capitol Hill areas were bustling with summer activity, though the broader state was dealing with ongoing drought concerns affecting agriculture and water resources.

NATIONAL NEWS (US)
The week of July 22-29 was dominated by several major stories: Trump administration immigration policies remained central, with controversy over family separation at the southern border continuing to drive national discourse. The stock market was performing strongly, with the S&P 500 near record highs. FBI Director Christopher Wray was testifying before Congress regarding election security and Russian interference in the 2016 election. The Brett Kavanaugh Supreme Court nomination process was underway, with hearings scheduled for early September. Cultural divisions over these political issues were running high across American congregations.

INTERNATIONAL NEWS
Several international stories intersected July 2018: Thailand's rescue of 13 soccer players and their coach from a flooded cave system (completed July 10) had captured global attention and recently concluded. The UK was navigating post-Brexit negotiations and Theresa May's continued tenure as Prime Minister amid ongoing political turbulence. Venezuela's humanitarian crisis was deepening with widespread shortages and political repression under Nicolas Maduro, driving migration into neighboring countries and the US.

CULTURAL MOMENT
American culture in late July 2018 was fractured along deep political lines, with partisan divisions evident even in entertainment and social media discourse. The Marvel film "Black Panther" cultural phenomenon was still reverberating from its February release. Sports attention was turning toward late-season baseball (MLB All-Star Game had just occurred July 17) and anticipation of football season. There was a pervasive sense of national discord and polarization that would have been present in nearly every community gathering, including churches like Saint John's Cathedral.
